summary: DeVault's October 2021 status update covers several projects in his free software ecosystem. The headline item is chat.sr.ht, a new SourceHut product built on Simon Ser's soju and gamja IRC projects, offering hosted IRC bouncing for paid users and webchat for anonymous users. His unnamed programming language (Hare) gained reflection capabilities, riscv64 support, and grammar improvements. He also highlights visurf (a vi-inspired Netsurf frontend), antiweb (a CSS toolkit for marginalized browsers), and Alpine Linux RISC-V work.
key_points: SourceHut is launching chat.sr.ht, a hosted IRC bouncer and webchat gateway, entering private beta with plans for general availability
The Hare programming language gained reflection, riscv64 support, date/time library work, and grammar improvements to reduce indentation
Antiweb aims to provide a CSS framework compatible with marginalized browsers like Netsurf and Lynx, with plans to migrate SourceHut's frontend to it
Visurf development continues with keyboard navigation and clipboard support, and DeVault actively solicits contributors of any skill level
Gemini tools gmnisrv and gmni reached 1.0 releases
money_quotes: The goal of this project is to provide a conservative CSS toolkit which allows you to build web interfaces which are compatible with marginalized browsers like Netsurf and Lynx.
chat.sr.ht is a hosted IRC bouncer which is being made available to all paid sr.ht users, and a kind of webchat gateway which will be offered to unpaid and anonymous users.
I should be able to migrate my blog to this framework in the foreseeable future, and ultimately the sourcehut frontend will be overhauled with this framework.
This project will need a lot of work to complete, and much of that work is very accessible to programmers of any skill level.
I have been working on Alpine Linux for RISC-V again, and have upstreamed the necessary patches to get u-Boot to bootstrap UEFI into grub for a reasonably sane boot experience.
key_insight: DeVault's monthly update reveals a consistent philosophy of building decentralized, accessible alternatives across the entire stack — from chat infrastructure and programming languages to browsers and CSS frameworks designed for the margins of the web.
